speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. Judy, you mentioned your father had a special job. Could you tell us a little more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well he was what was called an electrical engineer, and he got a job in that Department of the camp that fixed anything that had to do with electricity. As a matter of fact, I learned that he even was requested-- he was good-- to do this in the commandant's house, that the commandant asked for him when there was something wrong. He got the job through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whom-- my mother knew somebody who got him this job to begin with. I do remember that it took a certain amount of purchasing. In Westerbork, we were allowed to get packages. And I don't know who sent them. This might have been, you know, employees of my father, but we did get some food packages in Westerbork. And most of the content of this, my father took to his boss in order to stay in his good graces-- you know, that was money. It was also my father's job that part of the time kept us-- most of the time, kept us in Westerbork. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Not being placed on transport in Westerbork meant what you know, and who you knew, and where you were at the time of a transport. Transports left every Tuesday morning. Every Monday night they would come into the barracks, and there was this terrible silence. And they would read off the names of those who had to be on transport. And then, pandemonium broke out, and this was like every Monday for weeks and weeks, I remember this happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father immediately had a job. My mother worked in the laundry, and she also, on her day off, cleaned the house or the rooms of this couple who had lived there since the war broke out. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the children in those days-- because they had picked up so many Jewish people on June 20. It was such an influx into the camp into the population, they didn't know what to do with the kids. And so they took us out in the morning. They would count all the kids. We would go out, and we would sit outside of the camp in the heather all day.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember sitting there, and it was hot. And I remember sitting there, and it was cold and raining. And then they would count us again in the evening and take us back in-- long rove kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember sitting there sort of not moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children all live together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I lived with my-- I lived with my mother. It's just that all the kids were gathered. They gave us-- there were times when they gathered all the kids, and they gave us inoculations. I mean, this against terrible diseases-- typhus, and paratyphus, and cholera. They would actually inject the kids to prove-- the Germans did that-- to prove that how well they thought of children. And then a week later the kids would be on transport. I got sick. I got dysentery. I got the pneumonia. I got kidney infection. And I ended up in the hospital after about maybe three weeks in the camp. And I was in that hospital until January of 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were lots of quarantines in the hospital. There was a polio epidemic. I remember that I had hepatitis. And every time that there was anything contagious in the hospital, they quarantined the hospital. And during those times, I don't-- I did not see my parents. I remember there was one episode where somehow or other my father must have learned that everybody was in the hospital was going to go on transport. And in the middle of night, I was whisked out in a blanket. And I must have been with my mother that night, and the next morning I was brought back to the hospital. And it was very empty. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But it took until January until I was well enough to leave the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a very nice doctor. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish doctor. He was bent over. His name was Dr. Wolfe. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I mean, when I was sick, my treatment for the kidney infection was sugar water. Somehow or other, I don't know what that did. I remember a very restricted diet, like no salt. But I think we got a little better food than the rest of the people in the camp did. During the polio epidemic, it got so bad that they did a spinal puncture on everybody, and they gave us an apple for that. I remember that. I don't know how I spent my days. I really can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And after having been there so long, I got a corner bed, which felt a little bit more protected somehow or other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there other children in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, boys and girls. I remember there was one little boy who came in, and I knew him from Amsterdam. And he had polio, and he was very sick. And after the war, I saw his mom because she was hidden. And I wanted to tell her, and she didn't want to hear. I can understand that now why it must have been too painful for her to hear that. And he went to Auschwitz, sick and all. And-- In January, I was well enough to leave. And by that time, my mother had moved into a smaller room. I think there were only 16 beds for mothers and children. And we lived there for a while, and that's when I started going to school there. By then, the camp population was much, much smaller. And there was organized school. There was a Dutch school, and there was a German school. And there were very unfriendly relationships between the Dutch school and the German school. The only time I was hit during this camp period-- the Dutch kids and the German kids would have this not so friendly relationship. And there was a snowball fight, and the snowball broke a window in the German section of the school. And they told the principal that the Dutch kids did it, so all the Dutch kids, which was-- included myself, had to go in front of this principal and hold up our hands. And we got-- he had a twig. And we got a whipping in each hand. And I remember coming home and telling my parents that and, my father was incensed and went to the principal and said, don't you ever touch my child again. So my father said if anybody's going to hit my child it's going to be the parent. Although I remember very little spanking from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish principal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His name was-- no, I won't mention names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe any other punishment at Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just-- it was a very bare spot. And it wasn't paved spot, and it was-- when it rained, it was extremely muddy. And when it was dry, it was very sandy. And there was this railroad that sort of bisected the camp. It was like a central focus point. You couldn't escape it, because no matter where you went there it was. It really went through the whole camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We ended up getting a room just for my father, and my mother, and myself, which was a privilege. And I don't remember how long we lived in that room, probably maybe four or five months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In early September, there was a dictum-- oh, wait a minute. I want to tell you about my doll. My father was put in charge of making the purchases of electrical equipment that was needed for the camp. And they sent him to Amsterdam to get whatever the necessary stuff was. My mother and I stayed so they knew he'd come back. And somehow or other, he got a hold of my doll, and he brought it back. And I remember, you know, this doll coming back to me. I didn't even ask where did you go to get it. It just was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this doll was a very-- this doll stands for an awful lot of, I think, where my emotions went. Because I don't remember otherwise being overly upset or even crying a lot, in fact, probably none at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before going to the camp being very unhappy and even sometimes crying myself to sleep but during the camp, none of that. Early September, there was a dictum that everybody with children had to leave Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, September. And because my parents had me, we went and transport September 4 supposedly to Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we packed again, and I do remember that my mother had saved up some staples. And she had a suitcase with that, and then the rest-- we wore layers of clothes. I had summer clothes on, winter clothes on, and a ski suit. And my mother said I couldn't take the doll with. I was allowed to take the book but not the doll, because I had to have my hands free for carrying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were-- I had two friends, twins, who were not on transport. At the time, I didn't question it at all. I just knew they weren't on transport. And I went over there, and I said will you save my doll and give it back to me after the war? And they said yes, and so that's where my doll went.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were transported to the next camp in a cattle car. I don't re-- it took two days. The men took turns looking. There were way up high, there was a little bit of an opening. And they were trying to find out geographic locations to see where we really were going. I remember being-- throwing up. I still dread it. I remember just-- I don't know how long I vomited. But we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you vomit? You were in the middle of a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really-- it was, you know-- It Wasn't just-- there were over 70 people in that car. And I think there were times in the camp, probably a lot of times, where I just sort of tuned out. But I do remember the vomiting. And then when we arrived in this camp, which was Theresienstadt, they opened the door. And there was all the noises and all the shouts. And everybody had to leave the-- the cattle cars, and we weren't allowed to take anything with us. But I grabbed the book. And they let it go. You know, they didn't take it away from me. But all our luggage, the food that my mother had saved up-- all gone. And then there was again a processing kind of an experience, which I don't remember-- I do remember one thing of it. [COUGHS] We were told to strip, and we had to enter a very humid kind of a room and sit on bunks, like a wooden slotted benches. And we were told this was a de-lousing station. And the mothers and the children-- we sat there. And it was warm and humid, and it was ghastly quiet. And I didn't understand it, but it really felt very ominous. And then we have to sit there for I don't know how long, and then we got our original-- the clothes that we had on. We got those back. So I have my layers again. And I think that in retrospect, when I think about it now, some of the adults had some fear maybe even of gas at that time. Because it was such an eerie silence in that room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew about gassings at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, but I really wonder whether some of the adults did know. I honestly-- this was not anything that my mother and I talked about or my father and I. Then we were assigned to one of those stone barracks they have in Theresienstadt. And the camp was overpopulated, and so we got an attic where they just had straw on the floor. And we slept on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was they? Who where you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother. My father was in a different barrack. My mother got a job right away. She had to split mica into real, real thin shape. I still have some actually. And the better you produced, the more-- the less chance you had of losing that job. The kind of work you did in a camp was always very important, whether it was essential work or not. My father never even got a job in that camp. There was nothing available, so to speak. And I remember that I started not feeling good again in this camp and being very dizzy. And walking with my father and talking about this, and he said, don't tell mommy because she'll worry about you. So I didn't tell mommy. Then in the end of September, they started sending people on transports. And first they send all the men, and that included my father. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then they asked for-- they asked for volunteers of women and children to follow their husbands and the fathers. And there were actually some cards that arrived that said come and join us. But apparently my mother and father had made a pact that we were going to try to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the transports were going as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two expressions-- East and Poland was mentioned. But East was a word that really, you know, had a ring to it. The transport East-- nobody wanted to be on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just had bad vibes. People would volunteer to go to Bergen-Belsen and to Theresienstadt-- anything to stay off of transport East in Westerbork in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people know when you were in Westerbork what transport East was? Do you remember that as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just remember the feeling that went with the words of nobody wanted to be on a transport East. Now in Theresienstadt, Theresienstadt, even for the Jews during the war, had this connotation of being better than other places. And then it partially was. And so once you're there, you didn't really want to leave. Because the rumor had it that all the rest was worse. Bergen-Belsen-- people were happy in Westerbork to be able to be put on the Bergen-Belsen transport. That meant much better than East. In fact, I remember the first Bergen-Belsen transport from Westerbork was in regular trains, you know, with seats, not cattle cars. Germans were really masters at deception, and that includes the inoculation shots that we got. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother stayed in this job. I was loose. Then I-- What was your day like when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was disorganized. I just remember an ambling through the day without any structure. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with other children? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I don't remember child play at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have. Yeah, you had a food cart. You stand in line and the bread we got, a chunk, that had to last three days. And that usually meant that people ate it right away and then didn't have much for the other two. I remember some sort of-- it looked like dishwater soup. I don't remember spending a lot of-- I have not much memory of eating, hygiene. I don't remember having a toothbrush. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just-- that part is of laundering clothes-- the way I remember it is they weren't. After my father left, I got sicker and sicker. And I tried not to say anything to my mother, and then I guess I fainted a couple times. And so she took me to a doctor who tried to diagnose me and said come back next week. And then we'd come back next week, and he wasn't there anymore. And this went on and on. And finally a Dr. Kral diagnosed me with encephalitis, and so again I was hospitalized. And it was contagious, and in this-- like a big, big room-- were men, women, and there must have been some children. I was there. And I couldn't see my mother. And I was assigned a bed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which wasn't clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No fresh sheets. And I remember sort of telling myself that I must not move, to just lie in this same position, not turn, not move so that I wouldn't have to touch more of this sheet. And the only movement that I remember making is I knew that if you could bring your finger to your nose with your eyes closed that you were considered cured. And I practiced that over, and over, and over. And I don't know how long I was there, probably no more than three weeks. And then I was released. And at that point, my mother felt that I-- I was assigned work, which was for a woman whose job it was to mend the clothes together so that people could keep on wearing them. I still hate sewing. And my mother had no supervision over me during the day because-- I mean that's what she said. Because she had to work such long hours, and she was worried about my health. So she decided to put me in an orphanage, which I really resented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that inside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp. There was a-- there was one for Jewish boys and one for Jewish girls. It was like a big room with a caretaker assigned to it. And my mother felt that at least that way there'd be some supervision. There'd be somebody who would see to it that meals were adhered to and that I wouldn't be rambling around alone. | I stayed in this place until we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was camaraderie. We were all kids. We were in the same boat. We used to have games-- who could catch the most fleas during the night-- you know, like competition.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I got married, my husband once said, what was it like during the war in the camps? And I apparently told him it wasn't bad. The kids gave me a birthday party. Well, they actually-- when it was my birthday in the winter of 1945-- I remember getting some presents made out of mattress ticking, a bookmark. I don't have them anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also remember that there was a couple friends of my parents who stayed in Theresienstadt. They were elderly. They gave my mother, for my birthday, a book of matches which apparently had fantastic trading value. I don't know why or how, but that was like currency. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was this certain amount of companionship of being with other kids who-- we didn't talk about things, but we all knew what it was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a job. I worked. I was an errand girl. I brought messages from one office to another. I mean, there was a lot of the running of the camp was done by Jewish people. And they didn't have telephone, so the messages from office to office had to be conveyed by person. So it was called an ordinance, and that's what I was. In Westerbork, there was a period where we actually had school, and we had report cards, and stuff like that. It was very structured. There was nothing of that sort. It was totally prohibited to teach Jewish children. So there was no attempt made at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt-- not by the time that I was in there. It was earlier. Earlier, they had a very organized program for children but not-- OK.
